2754d1028e2699e66e9f2cdad8edb89769dac2c7:
name: qa-only  
preamble-tier: 4  
version: 1.0.0  
description: Report-only QA testing. (gstack)  
allowed-tools:  
  - Bash  
  - Read  
  - Write  
  - AskUserQuestion  
  - WebSearch  
triggers:  
  - qa report only  
  - just report bugs  
  - test but dont fix  
Systematically tests a web application and produces a structured report with health score, screenshots, and repro steps — but never fixes anything.  
Use when asked to "just report bugs", "qa report only", or "test but don't fix".  
For the full test-fix-verify loop, use /qa instead.  
Proactively suggest when the user wants a bug report without any code changes.  
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "bug report", "just check for bugs".  
Creates output directories for reports and screenshots in `.gstack/qa-reports/`.  
Automatically enters diff-aware mode if no URL is given and the user is on a feature branch.  
The primary mode for developers verifying their work is diff-aware mode.  
In diff-aware mode, the skill analyzes the branch diff to understand what changed.  
The skill identifies affected pages/routes from changed files in diff-aware mode.  
If no obvious pages/routes are identified from the diff, the skill falls back to Quick mode.  
The skill checks common local dev ports to detect the running app.  
In full mode, the skill systematically explores every reachable page and documents 5-10 well-evidenced issues.  
In quick mode, the skill performs a 30-second smoke test visiting the homepage and top 5 navigation targets.  
In regression mode, the skill compares the current run with a baseline to identify fixed and new issues.  
The health score is computed using a weighted average of category scores.  
The console category has a weight of 15% in the health score calculation.  
The links category has a weight of 10% in the health score calculation.  
Each category starts at 100 and deducts points based on the severity of issues found.  
The skill captures learnings from the session for future reference.  
The skill never fixes bugs; it only finds and documents them.  
If no test framework is detected, the report summary includes a note to run `/qa` to bootstrap one.  
The skill requires a one-time build of the browse binary if not already set up.  
The skill uses a report template from `qa/templates/qa-report-template.md` for documentation.  
The skill logs telemetry after workflow completion, including skill name, duration, and outcome.  
The skill outputs reports to both local and project-scoped locations.  
The skill captures screenshots for every issue found, ensuring evidence is documented.  
The skill checks the console for errors after every interaction during testing.  
The skill emphasizes depth over breadth, focusing on core features for thorough testing.  
The skill requires user input for CAPTCHA completion during authentication.  
The skill uses `snapshot -D` to show changes after actions taken during testing.  
The skill documents each issue immediately when found, appending to the report as it progresses.  
The skill checks for known bugs or issues in a `TODOS.md` file related to changed files.  
The skill verifies login success after filling out authentication forms.  
The skill uses `snapshot -i` to find navigation elements in single-page applications.  
The skill checks for framework-specific issues based on the detected framework during testing.  
The skill never reads source code; it tests as a user would interact with the application.  
The skill requires realistic data for testing, simulating user interactions end-to-end.  
The skill produces a health score and identifies the top 3 issues to fix at the end of testing.  
The skill saves a baseline for regression testing in `baseline.json` after each run.  
The skill logs insights and patterns discovered during testing for future sessions.  
The skill uses `snapshot -C` for tricky UIs to find clickable elements missed by the accessibility tree.  
The skill outputs structured reports with metadata, including date, duration, and framework used.  
The skill captures learnings only if they represent genuine discoveries that could save time in future sessions.  
The skill never deletes output files, ensuring all evidence is retained for review.  
The skill requires screenshots to be shown to the user after capturing them for transparency.  
The skill emphasizes the importance of verifying issues before documenting them.  
The skill uses `links` command to map navigation structure during the orientation phase.  
The skill checks for CSRF token presence in forms for Rails applications.  
The skill tests client-side navigation in Next.js applications to catch routing issues.  
The skill checks for mixed content warnings in WordPress applications.  
The skill monitors for memory leaks during extended use of single-page applications.  
The skill checks for N+1 query warnings in Rails applications during testing.  
The skill verifies Turbo/Stimulus integration in Rails applications for smooth page transitions.

The browse tool allows users to navigate URLs, interact with elements, verify page states, and take screenshots.

Commands can be executed in approximately 100 milliseconds per command.

The allowed tools for the browse skill include Bash, Read, and AskUserQuestion.

The skill can be invoked to perform actions like opening a page, testing a site, or taking a screenshot.

The skill can handle various tasks such as verifying page loads, testing user flows, and asserting element states.

Annotated screenshots can be taken to provide visual evidence for bug reports.

The tool supports testing responsive layouts and file uploads.

It can also compare different environments by diffing URLs.

The skill includes a command to find all clickable elements on a page.

The setup process for the browse tool requires a one-time build if not already set up.

The core QA patterns include verifying page loads, testing user flows, and visual evidence for bug reports.

The tool can render local HTML without needing an HTTP server.

The browse tool can be run in headed mode, which opens a visible Chromium window.

It supports proxy configurations for navigating through authenticated SOCKS5 or HTTP/HTTPS proxies.

The tool can handle user interactions that require manual input, such as CAPTCHAs or multi-factor authentication.

The snapshot command is used to capture the accessibility tree of a page, with various flags to customize the output.

Commands can be chained together for sequential execution.

28cc831cad350ef58904a59d71b9db1159ddb870:
The ce-doc-review is a tool designed to review requirements or plan documents using parallel persona agents.  
It surfaces role-specific issues to improve existing documents.  
The tool is invoked with the argument hint: "[mode:headless] [path/to/document.md]".  
In interactive mode, the platform question tool must be pre-loaded before any questions are asked.  
The `AskUserQuestion` tool is deferred and its schema is not available at session start.  
The numbered-list fallback applies only when there is no blocking question tool available.  
In headless mode, `safe_auto` fixes are applied silently, while other findings are returned as structured text.  
Headless mode requires a document path to function properly.  
If no document is specified in interactive mode, the user is prompted to select one.  
Document classification is based on content shape rather than file path.  
Requirements documents are identified by specific frontmatter fields and section headings.  
Plan documents are identified by different frontmatter fields and section headings.  
When content signals are mixed, the document path serves as a tie-breaker for classification.  
Conditional personas are activated based on specific signals found in the document content.  
The product-lens persona is activated for challengeable claims about what to build.  
The design-lens persona is activated for documents containing UI/UX references.  
The security-lens persona is activated for documents mentioning authentication or data handling.  
The scope-guardian persona is activated for documents with multiple priority tiers or large requirement counts.  
The adversarial persona is activated for documents with high-value challenge surfaces.  
The review team is announced to the user, detailing which personas will review the document.  
The review process includes dispatching subagents using bounded parallelism.  
Each subagent receives a full document and specific variables filled from the review context.  
The decision primer accumulates prior-round decisions for reference in subsequent rounds.  
Error handling ensures that the review continues even if a subagent fails or times out.  
The synthesis pipeline processes findings after all agents return, validating and resolving contradictions.  
The routing question in interactive mode offers four options for user decision-making.  
The tool's references include a subagent template and a findings schema for structured output.

gstack-upgrade is a skill designed to upgrade gstack to the latest version.  
The current version of gstack-upgrade is 1.1.0.  
The skill can be triggered by phrases like "upgrade gstack", "update gstack", or "get latest version".  
Voice triggers for the skill include "upgrade the tools", "update the tools", "gee stack upgrade", and "g stack upgrade".  
The skill detects whether gstack is installed globally or vendored before proceeding with the upgrade.  
If auto-upgrade is enabled, the skill will skip user confirmation and proceed directly to the upgrade.  
If auto-upgrade fails during the process, the previous version will be restored from a backup.  
The skill prompts the user with options to upgrade now, always keep updated, not now, or never ask again.  
If the user chooses to always keep updated, auto-upgrade will be enabled for future updates.  
The skill uses a snooze mechanism to delay upgrade prompts, with increasing durations for repeated snoozes.  
The installation type of gstack is determined by checking specific directory paths for git repositories.  
The old version of gstack is saved before the upgrade process begins.  
For git installs, the skill fetches the latest version and resets to the main branch.  
For vendored installs, the skill clones the latest version from GitHub and replaces the existing installation.  
If a local vendored copy exists and team mode is active, the skill removes the local copy to maintain a single source of truth.  
If team mode is not active, the local vendored copy is updated from the primary installation.  
Migration scripts are run after the upgrade to handle any necessary state fixes.  
The skill writes a marker indicating the old version and clears any cached update states.  
After the upgrade, the skill summarizes the changes in a user-friendly format.  
The skill continues with the user's original request after completing the upgrade process.  
When invoked directly, the skill forces a fresh update check to determine if an upgrade is available.  
If no upgrade is available, the skill checks for a stale local vendored copy and handles it accordingly.  
The skill provides feedback to the user about the status of the upgrade and any actions taken.
2bf35d79edf57a3108ef1939ff567a5706bae6e6:
ce-promote is a tool for drafting user-facing announcement and marketing copy for newly shipped features.

It can create various types of content, including X posts, changelog blurbs, LinkedIn posts, emails, blog intros, and demo scripts.

The tool is spiral-agnostic by default, meaning it can function without the Spiral CLI.

When the Spiral CLI is installed and authenticated, ce-promote can match the drafts to the user's brand voice.

The tool only drafts content and does not post, publish, commit, or open pull requests.

Users can invoke ce-promote with commands like "promote this," "draft the announcement," or "write the launch copy."

The command `/ce-promote` derives what shipped from context and drafts default announcements.

Users can provide a free-form description of the feature to guide the drafting process.

If specific channels are requested, such as "a tweet thread and a LinkedIn post," ce-promote will draft for those channels.

The tool summarizes the user-facing value of a feature in 1-3 sentences, focusing on what users can now do.

It derives information about the shipped feature from merged/active pull requests, diffs, changelogs, or recent commits.

If the tool cannot confidently determine what shipped, it will ask the user a clarifying question.

Default channels for announcements include an X post or short thread and a one-line changelog or release blurb.

The tool can scale the number of drafts based on the significance of the change and user requests.

To draft copy, ce-promote first checks the state of the Spiral CLI.

The Spiral CLI can be in one of three states: absent, ready, or unauthenticated.

If the Spiral CLI is absent, the tool offers to guide the user through the installation and setup process.

If the Spiral CLI is ready, ce-promote uses it to create voice-matched drafts.

If the Spiral CLI is unauthenticated, the tool prompts the user to log in or offers to draft without personalization.

The drafting process includes editorial and social media fundamentals to ensure strong copy.

Drafts are presented as clean, copy-pasteable blocks labeled by channel.

Each draft includes a session ID and a URL for users to open and edit in the Spiral web app if applicable.

Users can request revisions to the drafts, such as changes in tone, length, or angle.

The tool emphasizes that the drafts are for the user to review and ship themselves.
2ecbd80c49c51ddbd2ab6bd6698a3487c876b207:
Skill: test-driven-development (author: obra/superpowers).  
Write tests first, watch them fail, then implement the minimal code to pass.  
When to use: new features, bug fixes, refactoring, behavior changes.  
Workflow: red-green-refactor.  
Write a failing test to establish the requirement.  
Verify the test fails correctly ("If you didn't watch the test fail, you don't know if it tests the right thing").  
Write only the minimal code to pass.  
Refactor while keeping tests green.  
Rebuts common workarounds: testing afterward, manual verification, sunk-cost reasoning.  
Includes a verification checklist.

The document-release skill is designed for post-ship documentation updates. 

The skill triggers when documentation needs to be updated after code is shipped. 

The skill reads all project documentation and cross-references the code diff. 

The skill builds a Diataxis coverage map to assess documentation completeness. 

The skill updates README, ARCHITECTURE, CONTRIBUTING, and CLAUDE.md files to reflect changes. 

The skill detects architecture diagram drift and polishes the CHANGELOG voice. 

The skill cleans up TODOs and can optionally bump the version number. 

The skill surfaces documentation debt in the pull request body. 

The skill should be used when asked to "update the docs," "sync documentation," or "post-ship docs." 

The skill proactively suggests updates after a pull request is merged or code is shipped. 

The skill requires the current branch to not be the base branch to proceed. 

The skill gathers context about changes by analyzing the git diff and log. 

The skill discovers all documentation files in the repository for review. 

The skill classifies changes into categories relevant to documentation, such as new features or changed behavior. 

The skill outputs a summary of the number of files changed and documentation files to review. 

The skill builds a coverage map to identify critical gaps in documentation coverage. 

The skill assesses documentation coverage for new or changed public surface items. 

The skill flags items with zero coverage as critical gaps for further action. 

The skill detects architecture diagram drift by cross-referencing diagram entities against code changes. 

The skill requires reading the release-body section before auditing and applying updates. 

The skill emphasizes the importance of reading full content before editing any documentation file. 

The skill prohibits clobbering CHANGELOG entries and requires polishing wording only. 

The skill mandates asking for version bumps instead of making silent changes. 

The skill ensures every edit includes a one-line summary of what changed. 

The skill focuses on discoverability, ensuring every documentation file is reachable from README or CLAUDE.md. 

The skill flags stale architecture diagrams but does not auto-edit them. 

The skill maintains a friendly, user-forward voice in documentation updates.

gstack-openclaw-retro is a tool for generating weekly engineering retrospectives. 

The retrospective analyzes commit history, work patterns, and code quality metrics. 

The tool tracks persistent history and trends over time. 

gstack-openclaw-retro is team-aware and identifies the user running the command. 

The tool provides per-person contributions, praise, and growth areas. 

The default time window for analysis is the last 7 days. 

Users can specify different time windows using arguments like `24h`, `14d`, and `30d`. 

The `compare` argument allows users to compare the current window against a prior same-length window. 

All times reported are in the user's local timezone. 

For day units, the tool computes an absolute start date at local midnight. 

The tool fetches the origin and identifies the current user using git commands. 

The name returned by `git config user.name` is "you," referring to the person reading the retrospective. 

The tool runs multiple git commands to gather raw data for analysis. 

The metrics calculated include total commits, contributors, PRs merged, total insertions, and total deletions. 

The tool presents a per-author leaderboard showing contributions and areas of focus. 

The commit time distribution is shown as an hourly histogram in local time. 

The tool detects work sessions using a 45-minute gap threshold between consecutive commits. 

Commit types are categorized by conventional commit prefixes and shown as a percentage bar. 

The tool performs hotspot analysis to identify the most-changed files. 

PR sizes are estimated and categorized into small, medium, large, and XL buckets. 

The focus score measures the percentage of commits touching the most-changed top-level directory. 

The "Ship of the week" highlights the highest-LOC PR in the window. 

For each contributor, the tool computes total commits, LOC, areas of focus, and session patterns. 

The current user receives a detailed analysis including session analysis and focus score. 

Praise and opportunities for growth are provided for each teammate based on actual data. 

If no prior retros exist, the tool notes that it is the first retro recorded. 

The tool saves a JSON snapshot of the retrospective for future trend tracking. 

The narrative format for the retrospective is structured for Telegram. 

The tool compares metrics side-by-side when the user requests a comparison. 

All times are reported in the user's local timezone, and the tool never sets `TZ`. 

The retrospective saves history to the `memory/` directory for trend tracking. 

The completion status of the retrospective can be marked as DONE, DONE_WITH_CONCERNS, or BLOCKED.

376bc0594cc0675d60cb05e43ca8d73d409b14e2:
`ce-setup` is a tool for checking Compound Engineering health and repo-local configuration.

The tool reports optional tool capabilities and removes obsolete local configuration.

`ce-setup` helps refresh the configuration example and safely gitignore machine-local settings.

It is used for verifying setup, troubleshooting missing optional tools, or onboarding a repository.

The interaction method involves using platform-specific tools to ask user questions.

The tool does not bulk-install every optional dependency.

Missing tools are reported as optional capabilities for user installation.

The first step in the process is to determine the installed compound-engineering plugin version.

If the version cannot be determined, the version check is skipped.

The health check script is run if the skill directory can be resolved.

If the health check script is unavailable, inline checks are performed instead.

Inline checks include verifying the presence of optional tools like `agent-browser`, `gh`, `jq`, `ast-grep`, and `ffmpeg`.

If inside a git repository, the repo root is resolved using `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`.

The tool checks for obsolete `compound-engineering.local.md` at the repo root.

It checks whether `.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml` exists and if it is safely gitignored.

The tool compares `.compound-engineering/config.local.example.yaml` with `references/config-template.yaml`.

Diagnostic output is displayed to the user, with missing optional tools not counted as setup failures.

Phase 2 begins only if one or more repo-local project issues exist.

Project issues include an obsolete `compound-engineering.local.md`, unsafe `.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml`, or a missing/outdated `.compound-engineering/config.local.example.yaml`.

If no project issues exist, a success message is displayed.

The tool advises users to install optional tools only for the workflows they use.

In Phase 2, the repository root is resolved for all subsequent paths.

If `compound-engineering.local.md` exists, the user is asked if they want to delete it.

The example configuration is refreshed by copying `references/config-template.yaml` to the appropriate location.

If the bundled template cannot be located, the source template path is printed.

The user is asked if they want to create a local config file if it does not exist.

If approved, the local config file is created from the template.

The tool checks if `.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml` is gitignored.

If not, the user is offered to add it to `.gitignore`.

A summary is displayed at the end of the process, detailing fixes applied, skipped fixes, and missing optional tools.
37c2c96b20ec364e946b0407dc91ddbeea382cc3:
name: document-generate  
version: 1.0.0  
description: Generate missing documentation from scratch for a feature, module, or entire project.  
allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, AskUserQuestion  
triggers: write docs for this, generate documentation, document this feature, create a tutorial, write a how-to, explain this module, docs for this project  
Uses the Diataxis framework (tutorial / how-to / reference / explanation) to produce complete, structured documentation.  
Can be invoked standalone or called by /document-release when it finds coverage gaps.  
Use when asked to "write docs", "generate documentation", "document this feature", "create a tutorial", or "explain this module".  
The skill can be invoked two ways: standalone or from /document-release.  
The Diataxis framework consists of four quadrants: Tutorial, How-to, Reference, and Explanation.  
Tutorials are learning-oriented and walk a newcomer through a working example step-by-step.  
How-tos are task-oriented and show how to accomplish a specific goal, assuming basic familiarity.  
Reference documentation provides complete and accurate technical descriptions.  
Explanations clarify why things work the way they do.  
The philosophy is to research the whole before writing the parts, preventing incomplete documentation.  
Step 0 involves determining what to document based on the invocation context.  
AskUserQuestion is used to confirm scope and ask about documentation target options.  
The recommended output format is to create both inline summaries in existing files and deep docs in standalone files.  
If the project has a `docs/` directory, follow its conventions; otherwise, use plain Markdown files.  
Step 1 is the research phase, which involves mapping the project structure and reading key files.  
Key files to read include README.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, and main entry files like index.ts or app.py.  
Source code for each target entity must be read end-to-end, including tests and related modules.  
A concept map should be built before writing, outlining the target, purpose, key concepts, public surface, dependencies, dependents, edge cases, and design decisions.  
The output of the research phase should summarize the number of files researched, public surface items identified, concepts, and design decisions.  
Step 2 involves partitioning documentation into the Diataxis quadrants based on the entity type.  
A decision matrix helps determine which quadrants to produce for each entity type.  
If the documentation plan has more than five documents, AskUserQuestion to confirm before proceeding.  
Step 3 focuses on writing reference documentation first, as it establishes the vocabulary.  
Reference docs must be accurate, complete, and derived directly from code, including types, defaults, and constraints.  
Step 4 involves writing explanation documentation that answers "why does this work this way?"  
Explanation docs should lead with the problem, include diagrams, and name trade-offs explicitly.  
Step 5 is about writing how-to guides that are task-oriented and assume the reader knows the basics.  
How-to docs must include actionable steps, verification, and troubleshooting sections.  
Step 6 focuses on writing tutorials that are learning-oriented and take a newcomer from zero to a working example.  
Tutorials must allow for a visible result within three steps and include error paths.  
Step 7 involves cross-document linking and ensuring discoverability of all new documents.  
Entry-point files like README.md and CLAUDE.md must be updated to reference new docs.  
Step 8 is a quality self-review to ensure accuracy, completeness, and voice consistency.  
Step 9 includes committing the new documentation files and pushing them to the current branch.  
Documentation generated should include a summary of the scope, number of new and updated files, and coverage details.  
The skill emphasizes the importance of research before writing and accuracy in documentation.  
Completeness is prioritized over minimalism, encouraging comprehensive documentation.  
Cross-linking between different types of documentation is essential for discoverability.  
The voice of the documentation should be friendly, concrete, and user-forward, avoiding corporate or academic tones.
3999a2bf70030de97cfb9f09a71724a0b282a0ac:
The skill named "scrape" is designed to pull data from a web page. 

The version of the "scrape" skill is 1.0.0. 

The "scrape" skill is read-only and does not support mutating actions. 

The skill can be invoked using phrases like "scrape this page," "get data from," "pull from," "extract from," or "what is on." 

The skill first determines the user's intent based on their request. 

If the user's intent implies writing actions, the skill will refuse and direct them to use the "/automate" skill instead. 

The skill matches the user's intent to existing browser-skills and executes the appropriate one if a confident match is found. 

If no matching skill is found, the skill enters a prototype phase to drive the page using predefined primitives. 

The prototype phase includes navigating to the target URL, taking a snapshot of the page, pulling raw HTML, and gathering links. 

The skill emits results as JSON documents, typically structured as `{ "items": [...], "count": N }`. 

After a successful prototype, the skill suggests using "/skillify" to make the prototype a permanent skill. 

If the prototype fails to yield a sensible JSON shape, the skill reports the attempts and asks the user for further action. 

The skill does not handle authentication flows or multi-page crawls. 

The skill captures learnings from the session, logging insights for future reference. 

The skill's output is strictly JSON, with no additional prose unless requested by the user. 

The skill is part of the gstack toolset, which includes various other skills and functionalities.
3bc139827e8fd5e4750cde26a6ad1e5d60ea5d91:
The pair-agent skill allows a remote AI agent to pair with your browser.  
The current version of the pair-agent skill is 0.1.0.  
The skill generates a setup key for the remote agent to connect to your browser.  
The setup key expires in 5 minutes and can only be used once.  
The session token for the remote agent lasts 24 hours.  
The skill works with agents that can make HTTP requests, such as OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, and Cursor.  
The remote agent gets its own tab with scoped access by default.  
The skill can be invoked using voice triggers like "pair agent" or "share my browser."  
If the other agent is on the same machine, credentials are written directly to its config directory.  
If the other agent is on a different machine, an ngrok tunnel is required.  
The skill checks if the gstack browse server is running before pairing.  
The user is prompted to select which agent they want to pair with their browser.  
The user is asked whether the other agent is running on the same machine or a different one.  
If ngrok is installed and authenticated, the skill starts the tunnel automatically.  
If ngrok is not installed, the user is guided through the installation process.  
The skill provides detailed instructions for the user to copy and paste into the other agent.  
The remote agent can navigate URLs, click elements, fill forms, and take screenshots.  
With admin access, the remote agent can execute JavaScript and access cookies and storage.  
The skill includes troubleshooting steps for common issues like token expiration and rate limits.  
OpenClaw agents use the `exec` tool instead of Bash for command execution.  
Codex agents can execute shell commands via `codex exec`.  
Cursor's AI can run terminal commands directly.  
The skill allows revoking access to specific agents or all agents at once.
3c49969be22bdae28d057b56af69b2eebbe42324:
gstack-openclaw-retro is a tool for generating weekly engineering retrospectives.  
It analyzes commit history, work patterns, and code quality metrics.  
The tool tracks persistent history and trends over time.  
It provides per-person contributions, praise, and growth areas.  
Use it when asked for a weekly retro or to summarize what shipped that week.  
The default time window for analysis is the last 7 days.  
Users can specify different time windows: 24 hours, 14 days, or 30 days.  
The tool can compare the current window against a prior same-length window.  
All time reports are in the user's local timezone.  
For day units, the start date is computed at local midnight.  
The tool fetches the origin and identifies the current user using git commands.  
The current user is referred to as "you" in the retrospective.  
The tool runs multiple git commands to gather data on commits, authors, and file changes.  
It calculates metrics such as total commits, contributors, PRs merged, and LOC changes.  
The summary includes a leaderboard of contributors sorted by commit count.  
It displays a histogram of commit time distribution in local time.  
The tool detects coding sessions based on a 45-minute gap between commits.  
It categorizes commits by type using conventional commit prefixes.  
The tool performs hotspot analysis to identify frequently changed files.  
It estimates PR sizes and categorizes them into small, medium, large, and XL.  
The focus score measures the percentage of commits touching the most-changed directory.  
The "Ship of the Week" highlights the highest-LOC PR in the window.  
For each contributor, the tool computes total commits, LOC, and areas of focus.  
It provides a deep dive for the current user, including session analysis and focus score.  
Praise and growth opportunities for teammates are based on actual commits.  
If no prior retros exist, the tool notes that it's the first retro recorded.  
The tool saves a JSON snapshot of the retrospective for future reference.  
The narrative format for the retrospective is structured for Telegram.  
The tool can run in compare mode to show side-by-side metrics for different time windows.  
All times are reported in the user's local timezone, and markdown tables are avoided in the final output.  
Praise and growth areas are anchored in specific data from commits.  
The retrospective completion status can be marked as DONE, DONE_WITH_CONCERNS, or BLOCKED.
3c81b27fdbe4c4b03b78dc4cce2e09fe9080edea:
The ce-polish tool allows users to start a development server, open a feature in a browser, and iterate on improvements. Manual invocation is required to run ce-polish by typing `/ce-polish`. Users can provide a PR number or branch name as an argument, or leave it blank to use the current branch. The current branch must not be main or master.

To start the development server, the tool first checks for a `.claude/launch.json` configuration file. If the configuration file is found, it is used to start the project. If no launch.json is present, the tool auto-detects the project type by running a specific script. The project type determines which recipe reference to use for the start command and port defaults.

The tool supports various project types, including Rails, Next.js, Vite, Nuxt, Astro, Remix, SvelteKit, and Procfile. If the project type requires a package manager, the tool resolves it using a dedicated script. The port is also resolved through a specific script.

Once the server is started in the background, the tool probes the localhost for up to 30 seconds. If the server does not come up, the last 20 lines of the log are displayed, and the user is prompted for further action. The tool then opens the browser using the IDE's mechanism and informs the user that the dev server is running.

During the iteration phase, the user browses the feature and provides feedback for improvements. Changes made by the user trigger hot-reloading of the dev server. The user can request to check something, prompting the tool to use an agent-browser for screenshots or inspections. When the user indicates they are done, the tool commits the fixes and stops the process.

Reference files are loaded on demand, including launch.json schema, IDE detection, and dev-server documentation for various frameworks. Several scripts are invoked to read launch configurations, detect project types, resolve package managers, and determine port settings.
3cdb4b79507a28aa260a0bf7810fae94cef26e20:
name: qa  
preamble-tier: 4  
version: 2.0.0  
description: Systematically QA test a web application and fix bugs found. (gstack)  
allowed-tools:  
  - Bash  
  - Read  
  - Write  
  - Edit  
  - Glob  
  - Grep  
  - AskUserQuestion  
  - WebSearch  
triggers:  
  - qa test this  
  - find bugs on site  
  - test the site  
Runs QA testing, then iteratively fixes bugs in source code, committing each fix atomically and re-verifying.  
Use when asked to "qa", "QA", "test this site", "find bugs", "test and fix", or "fix what's broken".  
Proactively suggest when the user says a feature is ready for testing or asks "does this work?".  
Three tiers: Quick (critical/high only), Standard (+ medium), Exhaustive (+ cosmetic).  
Produces before/after health scores, fix evidence, and a ship-readiness summary.  
For report-only mode, use /qa-only.  
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "quality check", "test the app", "run QA".  
The skill requires a clean working tree for atomic commits.  
If the working tree is dirty, prompt the user to commit, stash, or abort.  
The skill can detect the running app by checking common local dev ports.  
If no local app is found, the skill will ask the user for the URL.  
The skill supports different modes: Full, Quick, Regression, and Diff-aware.  
In Diff-aware mode, the skill analyzes the branch diff to understand what changed.  
The skill can authenticate users via credentials or cookie files.  
The skill captures evidence of issues with screenshots and detailed descriptions.  
Each issue must be documented immediately when found, with evidence provided.  
The health score is computed based on console errors, broken links, and per-category scoring.  
The skill generates a structured report with findings, health scores, and recommendations.  
The report includes a summary of total issues found, fixes applied, and health score delta.  
If the repo has a TODOS.md, new deferred bugs will be added as TODOs.  
The skill logs learnings for future sessions based on non-obvious patterns or insights discovered.  
The skill requires a one-time setup for the browse binary if not already built.  
The skill can bootstrap a test framework if none is detected in the project.  
The skill generates regression tests for fixed issues if a test framework is available.  
The skill follows a strict one-commit-per-fix policy to maintain clear version history.  
The skill uses a health score rubric to evaluate the quality of the application being tested.  
The skill can search for relevant learnings from previous sessions to inform testing.  
The skill captures telemetry data after workflow completion for performance analysis.  
The skill can suggest improvements based on past learnings and user feedback.  
The skill is designed to test web applications like a real user, ensuring comprehensive coverage.  
The skill emphasizes the importance of verifying fixes and documenting findings thoroughly.  
The skill can handle various authentication methods, including 2FA and CAPTCHA.  
The skill is capable of generating CI/CD pipeline configurations for GitHub Actions.  
The skill can create output directories for reports and screenshots automatically.  
The skill can defer issues that cannot be fixed from source code, marking them accordingly.  
The skill can compute a final health score after all fixes are applied to ensure quality.  
The skill can update the CLAUDE.md file with testing expectations and procedures.  
The skill can log operational insights that improve future sessions and workflows.  
The skill is structured to ensure that all findings are actionable and well-documented.  
The skill is designed to be user-friendly, providing clear instructions and feedback throughout the process.  
The skill can adapt to different project structures and languages, ensuring flexibility in testing.  
The skill emphasizes the importance of user experience and functionality in web applications.  
The skill can provide a summary suitable for PR descriptions based on testing outcomes.  
The skill is built to enhance the QA process, making it more efficient and effective for developers.
3d043d4608a46541f8216572e98e91a6e81307b4:
Skill: tdd (author: mattpocock/skills).  
Test-driven development emphasizes behavior-focused tests through public APIs.  
TDD involves vertical slicing and incremental red-green-refactor cycles.  
Use TDD for new features where tests serve as specifications.  
TDD is beneficial for refactoring safely.  
TDD is suitable for systems with frequently changing implementation details.  
The TDD workflow begins with planning interface changes and prioritizing behaviors.  
Design for testability in the initial planning phase of TDD.  
Vertical slicing in TDD involves writing one test, implementing it, and repeating the process.  
In TDD, test behavior through public interfaces rather than implementation details.  
Refactor only after achieving a green test result in TDD.  
Refactoring in TDD can include extraction, creating deeper modules, and applying SOLID principles.  
Avoid horizontal slicing in TDD, as it leads to brittle test suites coupled to implementation details.
3e3e5ef889a4e64e1493e6cf3d6007cd342e2443:
The plan-devex-review is an interactive developer experience plan review tool from gstack. 

The tool is designed to improve developer experience by exploring developer personas and identifying friction points. 

The plan-devex-review has three modes: DX EXPANSION, DX POLISH, and DX TRIAGE. 

DX EXPANSION focuses on competitive advantage, while DX POLISH aims to bulletproof every touchpoint. 

DX TRIAGE addresses only critical gaps in the developer experience. 

The tool can be triggered by phrases like "DX review," "developer experience audit," or "API design review." 

The skill allows for various tools including Read, Edit, Grep, Glob, Bash, AskUserQuestion, and WebSearch. 

The skill's version is 2.0.0, and it is interactive. 

The skill emphasizes zero friction at the start, incremental steps, and learning by doing. 

It advocates for opinionated defaults with the option to override and aims to fight uncertainty in developer workflows. 

The skill includes a scoring rubric to evaluate developer experience on a scale from 0 to 10. 

The plan-devex-review requires a pre-review system audit to gather context about the developer-facing product. 

The audit includes reading the plan file, CLAUDE.md, README.md, and existing documentation. 

The tool also checks for existing DX-relevant content like getting started guides and error message patterns. 

If no design document is found, the tool offers to run the office-hours skill for sharper input. 

The tool auto-detects the product type based on the content of the plan. 

It uses a structured approach to gather evidence and force decisions before scoring the developer experience. 

The developer persona is identified to tailor the review process to specific user needs. 

An empathy narrative is created to simulate the developer's experience during onboarding. 

Competitive benchmarking is performed to understand how similar tools handle developer experience. 

The tool emphasizes the importance of magical moments in the developer journey. 

The review process includes tracing the developer journey and identifying friction points at each stage. 

The final output of the skill is a better plan that enhances the developer experience. 

The skill is designed to be rigorous and evidence-based, focusing on improving the developer experience rather than merely scoring it. 

The plan-devex-review is part of a broader effort to create a developer experience worth talking about.
3e7423a99e2dbf612d61bffc053e79ce4be84f96:
ce-commit is a tool for creating git commits with clear, value-communicating messages.  
It is activated by user commands like "commit", "commit this", or "save my changes".  
The tool produces well-structured commit messages that adhere to repository conventions when available.  
If no conventions exist, it defaults to the conventional commit format.  
The conventional commit format is structured as `type(scope): description`.  
The tool gathers context from git status, working tree diff, current branch, recent commits, and remote default branch.  
If the working tree is clean, the tool reports that there is nothing to commit.  
In a detached HEAD state, the user is prompted to create a feature branch.  
The remote default branch value is derived by stripping the `origin/` prefix.  
If the default branch cannot be resolved, it defaults to `main`.  
The commit message convention is determined by existing repo conventions, recent commit history, or defaults to conventional commits.  
When using conventional commits, the type should be chosen based on the most precise description of the change.  
Logical commits are created by scanning changed files for distinct concerns, grouping at the file level only.  
If the current branch is `main`, `master`, or the resolved default branch, a feature branch is automatically created before committing.  
The commit message should focus on the "why" rather than the "what" in the subject line.  
A body for the commit message is added for non-trivial changes, explaining motivation or trade-offs.  
Specific files should be staged by name to avoid including sensitive or unrelated changes.  
After committing, the tool runs `git status` to verify success and reports the commit hash and subject line.
3eec106069f2678e012720e2999528fc5c31be00:
The context-restore skill is designed to restore working context saved earlier by the /context-save command. 

The skill version is 1.0.0. 

The skill allows the use of tools such as Bash, Read, Glob, Grep, and AskUserQuestion. 

The skill can be triggered by phrases like "resume where I left off," "restore context," "where was I," "pick up where I left off," or "context restore." 

The skill loads the most recent saved state across all branches by default. 

The skill is useful for picking up work even across Conductor workspace handoffs. 

The skill should be paired with the /context-save command. 

The skill was formerly known as /checkpoint resume and was renamed due to Claude Code treating /checkpoint as a native rewind alias in current environments. 

The preamble for the skill includes commands to check for updates, create session directories, and manage session files. 

The skill checks for the current Git branch and session kind, echoing relevant information. 

The skill logs telemetry data if telemetry is enabled. 

The skill can handle plan mode operations, allowing certain commands that inform the plan. 

The skill can invoke AskUserQuestion to engage the user in decision-making. 

The skill can restore context from saved files, presenting a summary of the context, remaining work, and notes. 

If no saved contexts exist, the skill informs the user to run /context-save first. 

The skill emphasizes that it will never modify code and will only read saved files. 

The skill's context restoration process includes finding saved contexts, loading the appropriate file, and presenting the summary. 

The skill offers next steps to the user after presenting the context summary. 

The skill maintains a focus on cross-branch context restoration, allowing users to resume work regardless of the current branch. 

The skill's output includes details such as the title, branch, timestamp, duration, status, summary, remaining work, and notes from the saved context. 

The skill is part of the gstack suite and is not a built-in feature of Claude Code.

44bd065fba3f6fc9dbd2e29b99ae79738e438b37:
ce-proof is a tool for publishing, viewing, commenting on, and editing markdown documents via Proof (proofeditor.ai).

Proof supports two modes: Web API for HTTP document management and Local Bridge for macOS app interaction.

Every write to a Proof document must be attributed with a Machine ID (`ai:compound-engineering`) and a Display Name (`Compound Engineering`).

The primary use case for ce-proof is one-way publishing of local markdown files, creating a shareable URL without syncing back to disk.

Users can request to share a document with phrases like "share this to proof" or "publish this to proof".

The Web API allows creating a shared document without authentication, returning a shareable URL and access token.

To create a shared document, send a POST request to `https://www.proofeditor.ai/share/markdown` with a JSON body containing the title and markdown content.

The response from creating a shared document includes a slug, token URL, access token, and links for state and operations.

To read a shared document, fetch the URL directly with content negotiation, specifying the desired response format (JSON or markdown).

Editing a shared document involves sending operations to `POST https://www.proofeditor.ai/api/agent/{slug}/ops` or using `/api/agent/{slug}/edit/v2` for block edits.

Every mutation requires a `baseToken`, which can be obtained from the most recent state or snapshot read.

The editing strategy should avoid whole-document rewrites, preferring narrow edit primitives like `find_replace_in_doc` or block operations.

The Local Bridge requires the Proof app running and communicates via `http://localhost:9847`.

Key endpoints for the Local Bridge include `/windows`, `/state`, `/marks`, and various POST endpoints for commenting and suggesting edits.

Presence statuses in the Local Bridge include `thinking`, `reading`, `idle`, `acting`, `waiting`, and `completed`.

To review a shared document, extract the slug and token from the URL, read the document, and perform edits using the appropriate endpoints.

When creating and sharing a new document, read the local markdown file and JSON-encode its contents before sending it to the Proof API.

To pull a Proof document to local storage, read the current state and write the markdown to a local file atomically.

Safety measures include using `/state` as the source of truth, preferring block-level edits during collaboration, and ensuring consistent attribution in all operations.

A code-reviewer subagent should be dispatched to catch issues before they cascade. 

Code reviews are mandatory after each task in subagent workflows. 

Code reviews are mandatory after major features are completed. 

Code reviews are mandatory before merging to the main branch. 

Code reviews can be optionally requested when a developer is stuck. 

Code reviews can be optionally requested before refactoring code. 

Code reviews can be optionally requested after fixing a complex bug. 

The workflow involves dispatching a specialized review agent with precise implementation details, requirements, and the commit range. 

Session history should not be included when dispatching the review agent. 

The reviewer should focus on the work product during the code review. 

Feedback from code reviews should be categorized into Critical, Important, and Minor. 

Critical feedback indicates issues that must be fixed immediately. 

Important feedback indicates issues that should be resolved before proceeding. 

Minor feedback indicates notes that can be addressed later. 

The core principle of code reviews is that early and frequent review prevents issues from compounding.
4d16dfd9799c3dd3edea574a1445acaab55ef0c0:
The `ce-test-browser` is designed to run browser tests on pages affected by a current PR or branch. 

The `agent-browser` CLI is the exclusive tool for browser automation in this workflow. 

Users should not use alternative browser automation systems or built-in browser-control tools. 

The `agent-browser` CLI can be used for opening pages, clicking elements, filling forms, taking screenshots, and scraping rendered content. 

A local development server must be running to use the `ce-test-browser`. 

The `agent-browser` CLI must be installed for the `ce-test-browser` to function. 

To check if `agent-browser` is installed, run the command `command -v agent-browser >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "Installed" || echo "NOT INSTALLED"`. 

If `agent-browser` is not installed, users should run `/ce-setup` for the current install command. 

Before starting tests, users must verify that `agent-browser` is available. 

In pipeline mode, the process skips asking the user about browser mode and defaults to headless. 

In manual mode, users are prompted to choose between headed or headless mode for running tests. 

If a PR number is provided, the command `gh pr view [number] --json files -q '.files[].path'` is used to determine the test scope. 

If 'current' or empty is provided, the command `git diff --name-only main...HEAD` is used to determine the test scope. 

If a branch name is provided, the command `git diff --name-only main...[branch]` is used to determine the test scope. 

Changed files are mapped to testable routes based on specific patterns. 

In pipeline mode, a free port must be detected, and the assumption that port 3000 is available should not be made. 

In manual mode, the preferred port is used directly without scanning for alternatives. 

The preferred port is determined by checking for an explicit argument, project instructions, `package.json`, environment files, and defaults to 3000. 

In pipeline mode, if no server is listening on the preferred port, a server is started automatically in the background. 

In manual mode, users are informed to start their development server if it is not running. 

The command `agent-browser open http://localhost:${PORT}` is used to open the development server in the browser. 

For each affected route, the command `agent-browser open "http://localhost:${PORT}/[route]"` is used to navigate and capture a snapshot. 

In headed mode, the command `agent-browser --headed open "http://localhost:${PORT}/[route]"` is used to navigate. 

Key elements on the page must be verified, including the presence of the page title, primary content, and expected form fields. 

Critical interactions can be tested using the command `agent-browser click @e1`. 

Screenshots can be taken using the command `agent-browser screenshot page-name.png`. 

Human verification is required for flows that need external interaction, such as OAuth or payments. 

When a test fails, the failure must be documented with a screenshot and reproduction steps. 

Users are prompted on how to proceed after a test failure, with options to fix or skip the test. 

After all tests are complete, a summary of the test results is presented, including the test scope, pages tested, and any failures. 

Quick usage examples for the `ce-test-browser` include testing the current branch changes, a specific PR, a specific branch, or on a specific port. 

The `agent-browser` CLI has various commands for navigation, snapshots, interactions, screenshots, and waiting for elements.

gstack-upgrade is a skill designed to upgrade gstack to the latest version.  
The current version of gstack-upgrade is 1.1.0.  
gstack-upgrade can be triggered by phrases like "upgrade gstack" or "get latest version."  
The allowed tools for gstack-upgrade include Bash, Read, Write, and AskUserQuestion.  
gstack-upgrade detects whether gstack is installed globally or vendored.  
If auto-upgrade is enabled, gstack will upgrade without user confirmation.  
If auto-upgrade fails, gstack will restore the previous version from a backup.  
gstack-upgrade prompts the user for confirmation before proceeding with the upgrade if auto-upgrade is not enabled.  
The user can choose to always keep gstack up to date or to snooze the upgrade reminder.  
The install type of gstack is determined by checking specific directory paths for git repositories.  
gstack-upgrade saves the old version of gstack before performing the upgrade.  
For git installs, gstack-upgrade fetches the latest changes and resets to the main branch.  
For vendored installs, gstack-upgrade clones the latest version of gstack from GitHub.  
If a local vendored copy exists and team mode is active, gstack-upgrade removes the local copy.  
If a local vendored copy exists and team mode is not active, gstack-upgrade updates the local copy from the primary install.  
gstack-upgrade runs migration scripts after the setup completes to handle state fixes.  
gstack-upgrade writes a marker file indicating the old version after the upgrade.  
gstack-upgrade reads the CHANGELOG.md to summarize new features and changes after the upgrade.  
After showing the new features, gstack-upgrade continues with the user's original skill invocation.  
When invoked directly, gstack-upgrade forces a fresh update check to determine if an upgrade is available.  
If no upgrade is available, gstack-upgrade checks for a stale local vendored copy.  
If the local vendored copy is stale and team mode is active, gstack-upgrade removes it.  
If the local vendored copy is stale and team mode is not active, gstack-upgrade updates it from the primary install.  
If the local vendored copy is up to date, gstack-upgrade informs the user that they are on the latest version.

Gauntlet AI offers a fellowship program designed to help participants design and ship production-grade AI systems.  
The fellowship lasts for 10 weeks, consisting of 3 weeks remote and 7 weeks in-person in Austin, Texas.  
Participants receive weekly assignments on Monday and must ship their work by Friday, with no extensions allowed.  
The curriculum is divided into two phases: Phase 1 is remote and covers AI coding methodologies, legacy systems, and open-source AI agents.  
Phase 2 is on-site and includes projects for hiring partners, enterprise systems, legacy modernization, multimodal AI, and capstone projects.  
Travel, housing, food, compute, and model access are covered by hiring partners, and no tuition is charged to participants.  
Graduates of the program secure positions with starting compensation ranging from $200k to $950k.  
Eligibility for Gauntlet Prime requires 3+ years of professional software engineering experience and U.S. work authorization.  
Eligibility for Gauntlet for America requires 1+ year of professional software engineering experience and U.S. citizenship.  
The selection philosophy emphasizes judging candidates based on their output rather than their educational background.  
Cohort 5 starts on April 27, 2026, Cohort 6 starts on July 6, 2026, and Cohort 7 starts on September 14, 2026.  
Applications for the fellowship are reviewed on a rolling basis.  
Matthew Daw is a current participant in Cohort V (Cohort 5).

`ce-product-pulse` generates a time-windowed pulse report on user experiences and product performance. 

The report includes metrics on usage, quality, errors, and signals worth investigating. 

Users can request a pulse report by saying phrases like "run a pulse" or "weekly recap." 

Reports are saved to `docs/pulse-reports/` and key points are surfaced in chat. 

The skill does not mutate the product, database, or any external system. 

Pulse settings are appended to `.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml`, which is machine-local and gitignored. 

The default lookback window for the pulse report is 24 hours unless specified otherwise. 

A 15-minute trailing buffer is applied to the upper bound of the time window to account for ingestion lag. 

The report is designed to be a single page, targeting 30-40 lines of output. 

No personally identifiable information (PII) is included in saved reports. 

Analytics and tracing queries run in parallel, while database queries run serially to avoid load. 

The skill requires read-only database access if a database is used as a data source. 

If `STRATEGY.md` exists, the skill reads it before asking questions to seed the product name and key metrics. 

The first-run interview captures essential configuration details such as product name and primary engagement event. 

The captured configuration is written to `<repo-root>/.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml`. 

The skill offers to set up a recurring run for the pulse report after the first-run interview. 

Pulse reports are assembled using a template that includes headlines, usage metrics, system performance, and follow-ups. 

Reports are saved with a timestamp in the format `YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM.md`. 

The skill does not report on what shipped, set thresholds, or alert users. 

The saved-reports folder serves as a team's working memory, allowing past pulses to be grepable and diffable.

ce-ideate is a tool designed to generate and evaluate grounded ideas about a specific topic.

The current year is 2026, which is relevant for dating ideation documents.

ce-ideate precedes ce-brainstorm in the ideation workflow.

ce-ideate answers the question: "What are the strongest ideas worth exploring?"

ce-brainstorm answers the question: "What exactly should one chosen idea mean?"

ce-plan answers the question: "How should it be built?"

The workflow produces a ranked ideation artifact, which is written to `docs/ideation/` or a temporary path if that directory is not present.

ce-ideate does not produce requirements, plans, or code.

The interaction method involves using specific tools to ask users questions, ensuring one question is asked at a time.

The focus hint can include concepts, paths, research artifacts, constraints, or volume hints.

If no argument is provided, ce-ideate proceeds with open-ended ideation.

The core principles include grounding before ideating, generating many ideas, critiquing all, and routing action into brainstorming.

The model tiers include extraction, generation, and ceiling tiers, with specific guidelines for when to use each.

The degradation rule states that when model selection is not supported, everything is dispatched on the inherited model.

The execution flow includes phases for resuming and scoping, checking for recent ideation work, and identifying the subject of ideation.

The subject-identification gate ensures the subject of ideation is identifiable before proceeding.

The mode classification determines whether the subject is repo-grounded, elsewhere-software, or elsewhere-non-software.

The context-substance gate gathers necessary context for ideation, especially in elsewhere modes.

The focus and volume interpretation phase infers the context and any volume overrides from the arguments provided.

Cost transparency notice informs users about the agent count and cost shape for the inferred mode before dispatching.

Phase 1 involves mode-aware grounding, gathering context and evidence based on the mode chosen.

Web research is always on unless explicitly skipped by the user.

User-supplied research artifacts are treated as evidence and are processed accordingly.

The consolidated grounding summary compiles results from all dispatched agents into a coherent overview.

Phase 1.5 decomposes the topic into 3-5 orthogonal axes to ensure comprehensive coverage during ideation.

Evidence scouts gather specific information about each axis in repo mode.

Phase 2 focuses on divergent ideation, generating a full candidate list before critiquing any ideas.

The ideation process includes a post-merge synthesis and checkpoint steps to ensure quality and coherence in the final output.

name: autoplan  
preamble-tier: 3  
version: 1.0.0  
description: Auto-review pipeline — reads the full CEO, design, eng, and DX review skills from disk and runs them sequentially with auto-decisions using 6 decision principles. (gstack)  
benefits-from: [office-hours]  
triggers:  
  - run all reviews  
  - automatic review pipeline  
  - auto plan review  
allowed-tools:  
  - Bash  
  - Read  
  - Write  
  - Edit  
  - Glob  
  - Grep  
  - WebSearch  
  - AskUserQuestion  
When to invoke this skill: Surfaces taste decisions at a final approval gate. One command, fully reviewed plan out. Use when asked to "auto review", "autoplan", "run all reviews", "review this plan automatically", or "make the decisions for me". Proactively suggest when the user has a plan file and wants to run the full review gauntlet without answering 15-30 intermediate questions.  
Voice triggers: "auto plan", "automatic review".  
Plan Mode Safe Operations: In plan mode, allowed because they inform the plan: `$B`, `$D`, `codex exec`/`codex review`, writes to `~/.gstack/`, writes to the plan file, and `open` for generated artifacts.  
Skill Invocation During Plan Mode: If the user invokes a skill in plan mode, the skill takes precedence over generic plan mode behavior. Treat the skill file as executable instructions, not reference. Follow it step by step starting from Step 0; the first AskUserQuestion is the workflow entering plan mode, not a violation of it.  
If `PROACTIVE` is `"false"`, do not auto-invoke or proactively suggest skills. If a skill seems useful, ask: "I think /skillname might help here — want me to run it?"  
If output shows `UPGRADE_AVAILABLE <old> <new>`: read `~/.claude/skills/gstack/gstack-upgrade/SKILL.md` and follow the "Inline upgrade flow".  
If output shows `JUST_UPGRADED <from> <to>`: print "Running gstack v{to} (just updated!)".  
Feature discovery, max one prompt per session: AskUserQuestion for Continuous checkpoint auto-commits.  
If `LAKE_INTRO` is `no`: say "gstack follows the **Boil the Ocean** principle".  
If `TEL_PROMPTED` is `no` AND `LAKE_INTRO` is `yes`: ask telemetry once via AskUserQuestion.  
If `PROACTIVE_PROMPTED` is `no` AND `TEL_PROMPTED` is `yes`: ask once about proactive suggestions.  
If `HAS_ROUTING` is `no` AND `ROUTING_DECLINED` is `false` AND `PROACTIVE_PROMPTED` is `yes`: Check if a CLAUDE.md file exists in the project root.  
If `VENDORED_GSTACK` is `yes`, warn once via AskUserQuestion about vendoring.  
If `SPAWNED_SESSION` is `"true"`, do NOT use AskUserQuestion for interactive prompts.  
AskUserQuestion Format: "AskUserQuestion" can resolve to two tools at runtime: the **host MCP variant** or the **native** Claude Code tool.  
When AskUserQuestion is unavailable or a call fails: Tell three outcomes apart: Auto-decide denial, genuine failure, and fallback to prose.  
Phase 0: Intake + Restore Point: Save the plan file's current state to an external file.  
Phase 1: CEO Review: Follow plan-ceo-review/SKILL.md — all sections, full depth. Override: every AskUserQuestion → auto-decide using the 6 principles.  
Phase 2: Design Review: Follow plan-design-review/SKILL.md — all 7 dimensions, full depth.  
Phase 3: Eng Review: Follow plan-eng-review/SKILL.md — all sections, full depth.  
Phase 3.5: DX Review: Follow plan-devex-review/SKILL.md — all 8 DX dimensions, full depth.  
Final Approval Gate: Present the aggregated per-phase task lists.  
Implementation Tasks aggregator: Aggregate the per-phase task lists each review skill wrote.  
Decision Audit Trail: After each auto-decision, append a row to the plan file.  
Pre-Gate Verification: Before presenting the Final Approval Gate, verify that required outputs were actually produced.  
Completion: Write Review Logs: On approval, write 3 separate review log entries.  
Important Rules: Never abort. The user chose /autoplan. Respect that choice.

ce-promote is a tool for drafting user-facing announcement and marketing copy for newly shipped features. 

ce-promote can create copy for various channels, including X posts, changelog blurbs, LinkedIn posts, emails, blog intros, and demo scripts. 

ce-promote is spiral-agnostic by default, meaning it can function without the Spiral CLI installed. 

When the Spiral CLI is installed and authenticated, ce-promote drafts are voice-matched to the user's brand. 

ce-promote only drafts content and does not post, publish, commit, or open pull requests. 

The user can invoke ce-promote by using phrases like "promote this," "draft the announcement," or "write the launch copy." 

To determine what feature has shipped, ce-promote can use a free-form description provided by the user or derive information from context. 

If the user does not provide a description, ce-promote can gather information from merged or active pull requests, the diff of changes, the changelog, or recent commits. 

The output of ce-promote includes a summary of the user-facing value of the feature, focusing on what users can now do. 

ce-promote defaults to drafting an X post or a one-line changelog blurb but can scale to include additional channels as requested by the user. 

To draft copy, ce-promote first checks the state of the Spiral CLI to determine if it is ready for use. 

If the Spiral CLI is absent, ce-promote offers to set it up once unless the user has opted out previously. 

If the Spiral CLI is unauthenticated, ce-promote prompts the user to log in or offers to guide them through the installation process. 

When the Spiral CLI is ready, ce-promote uses it to create drafts that match the user's brand voice. 

If the Spiral CLI is not available, ce-promote drafts copy using a compact layer of editorial and social media expertise. 

ce-promote emphasizes leading with the user-facing outcome in all drafted content. 

Drafts for social media channels must hook the reader in the first line and match the native shape and length of each channel. 

Each draft produced by ce-promote is presented as a clean, copy-pasteable block labeled by channel. 

ce-promote allows users to request revisions to the drafts, including changes in tone, length, or angle. 

The drafts created by ce-promote are for the user to review and ship themselves, reinforcing that posting is a human action.

Careful is a safety tool designed to provide guardrails for destructive commands.  
The current version of Careful is 0.1.0.  
Careful warns users before executing potentially destructive commands.  
It is invoked when users request "be careful," "safety mode," or similar phrases.  
Destructive commands include `rm -rf`, `DROP TABLE`, `git push --force`, and `kubectl delete`.  
Users can override warnings issued by Careful.  
Careful is particularly useful when working in production or shared environments.  
The tool checks every bash command for destructive patterns before execution.  
If a destructive command is detected, Careful issues a warning and allows the user to cancel.  
The command to check for destructive patterns is located at `$HOME/.claude/skills/gstack/careful/bin/check-careful.sh`.  
Careful logs usage analytics to `~/.gstack/analytics/skill-usage.jsonl`.  
The patterns protected by Careful include recursive deletes and data loss commands.  
Allowed exceptions for Careful include deleting specific directories like `node_modules` and `.next`.  
Careful's hooks are session-scoped and can be deactivated by ending the conversation.  
The tool supports Bash and Read as allowed tools.  
Careful's warning message is returned as `permissionDecision: "ask"` when a match is found.
5749721c88b626bc899afa63c476bf8bafd76384:
Form 1040 (Simplified) is used for filing a U.S. individual income tax return for tax year 2025.  
The guide provides instructions for wage earners whose only income is from W-2 jobs.  
Line 1a requires entering the amount from Box 1 of the W-2, which includes wages, tips, and other compensation.  
If there are multiple W-2s, the amounts in Box 1 should be added together.  
Line 9 represents total income, which equals Line 1a for returns with only W-2 wages.  
Line 11 indicates that adjusted gross income (AGI) equals Line 9 if there are no above-the-line adjustments.  
Line 12 specifies the standard deduction amounts for tax year 2025 based on filing status.  
The standard deduction for single filers is $15,750 for tax year 2025.  
The standard deduction for married filing jointly is $31,500 for tax year 2025.  
The standard deduction for head of household is $23,625 for tax year 2025.  
The standard deduction for married filing separately is $15,750 for tax year 2025.  
Line 15 calculates taxable income by subtracting Line 12 from Line 11, with a minimum of zero.  
Line 16 requires computing tax on taxable income using the 2025 tax-rate schedule for the filing status.  
The 2025 tax brackets for single filers and married filing separately start at 10% for income from $0 to $11,925.  
The 2025 tax brackets for single filers and married filing separately apply 12% on income over $11,925 up to $48,475.  
The 2025 tax brackets for single filers and married filing separately apply 22% on income over $48,475 up to $103,350.  
The 2025 tax brackets for single filers and married filing separately apply 24% on income over $103,350 up to $197,300.  
The 2025 tax brackets for married filing jointly start at 10% for income from $0 to $23,850.  
The 2025 tax brackets for married filing jointly apply 12% on income over $23,850 up to $96,950.  
The 2025 tax brackets for married filing jointly apply 22% on income over $96,950 up to $206,700.  
The 2025 tax brackets for married filing jointly apply 24% on income over $206,700 up to $394,600.  
The 2025 tax brackets for head of household start at 10% for income from $0 to $17,000.  
The 2025 tax brackets for head of household apply 12% on income over $17,000 up to $64,850.  
The 2025 tax brackets for head of household apply 22% on income over $64,850 up to $103,350.  
The 2025 tax brackets for head of household apply 24% on income over $103,350 up to $197,300.  
Line 25 requires entering the amount from Box 2 of the W-2, which indicates federal income tax withheld.  
Line 33 represents total payments, which equals Line 25 for a wage-only return.  
Line 34 calculates overpayment or refund by subtracting Line 16 from Line 33 if total payments exceed tax.  
Line 37 indicates the amount owed if Line 16 exceeds Line 33, calculated by subtracting Line 33 from Line 16.  
A completed return must clearly state filing status, total income, AGI, standard deduction taken, taxable income, computed tax, total federal tax withheld, and the final refund or amount owed.
584ce344d6e5919ff70564d4242041326a2d10d8:
name: health  
preamble-tier: 2  
version: 1.0.0  
description: Code quality dashboard. (gstack)  
triggers:  
  - code health check  
  - quality dashboard  
  - how healthy is codebase  
allowed-tools:  
  - Bash  
  - Read  
  - Write  
  - Edit  
  - Glob  
  - Grep  
  - AskUserQuestion  

The health skill wraps existing project tools to compute a weighted composite score from 0-10.  
The skill tracks trends over time and is invoked with phrases like "health check" or "run all checks."  
The skill uses tools such as type checker, linter, test runner, dead code detector, and shell linter.  
The skill's preamble includes commands to check for updates and manage session files.  
The skill can operate in plan mode, allowing certain commands that inform the plan.  
If the user invokes a skill in plan mode, it takes precedence over generic plan mode behavior.  
The skill can suggest or invoke commands based on user input and configuration settings.  
The skill logs telemetry data after execution, including session duration and outcomes.  
The skill generates a health dashboard that summarizes code quality metrics.  
The dashboard includes scores for type checking, linting, testing, dead code detection, and shell linting.  
Each category in the dashboard is scored on a scale from 0 to 10 based on specific criteria.  
The composite score is calculated by weighting individual category scores according to their importance.  
The skill persists health check results to a history file for trend analysis.  
If a tool is not available, it is recorded as skipped, and the weight is redistributed among remaining categories.  
The skill presents a clear table of results, including project name, branch, date, and individual category scores.  
If any category scores below 7, the skill lists the top issues from that tool's output.  
The skill provides recommendations for improving code quality based on the scores and identified issues.  
The skill respects the configuration in CLAUDE.md, using specified commands if a health stack section is present.  
The skill does not fix issues but presents findings and recommendations for user action.  
The skill captures the last 10 health check entries for trend analysis and improvement suggestions.  
The skill's output includes a summary of regressions detected if the score drops compared to the previous run.  
The skill emphasizes the importance of completeness and accuracy in reporting code quality metrics.  
The skill is designed to help teams track code quality over time and make informed decisions about improvements.
58f322611b1321c106a21edab0e0e21a1d808b4d:
The skill is named "cso" and is at version 2.0.0.  
It is designed for conducting security audits as a Chief Security Officer.  
The skill allows the use of tools such as Bash, Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Agent, WebSearch, and AskUserQuestion.

The default mode runs all phases (0-14) with an 8/10 confidence gate.  
The comprehensive mode runs all phases (0-14) with a 2/10 confidence gate.  
Scope flags are mutually exclusive; using multiple will result in an error.  
The skill emphasizes the importance of auditing dependencies rather than just the code itself.  
It produces a Security Posture Report with findings, severity ratings, and remediation plans.  
The skill includes a phase for architecture mental model and stack detection to understand the tech stack.  
It uses the Grep tool for all code searches to find vulnerabilities.  
The attack surface census maps both code and infrastructure surfaces.
